questions.questionText: The neurotransmitter at the neuromuscular junction is:
questions.optionB: Acetylcholine
questions.explanation: ACh is released from the motor nerve terminal at the NMJ, binds to nicotinic receptors on the motor end plate → EPP → muscle AP → contraction. ACh is rapidly degraded by acetylcholinesterase. Myasthenia gravis: antibodies against nAChR. Lambert-Eaton: antibodies against presynaptic Ca2+ channels. Reference: Guyton — Neuromuscular Junction.

questions.questionText: The sequence of events in skeletal muscle contraction (excitation-contraction coupling) is:
questions.optionA: AP → T-tubule → SR Ca2+ release → troponin binding → cross-bridge cycling
questions.optionB: Ca2+ release → AP → contraction
questions.explanation: EC coupling: muscle AP propagates along sarcolemma → T-tubules (invaginations) → DHPR (L-type Ca2+ channel, voltage sensor) activates RyR1 on SR → Ca2+ released from SR → Ca2+ binds troponin C → tropomyosin shifts → exposing actin binding sites → myosin cross-bridge cycling → contraction. Relaxation: Ca2+ pumped back to SR by SERCA pump. Reference: Guyton — EC Coupling.

questions.questionText: In the sliding filament theory, during contraction:
questions.optionA: Both thick and thin filaments shorten
questions.optionB: Thin filaments slide over thick filaments; filament length remains constant
questions.optionD: The entire sarcomere dissolves and reforms
questions.explanation: Sliding filament theory: during contraction, actin (thin) filaments slide over myosin (thick) filaments toward the M-line. Individual filament lengths do NOT change. The sarcomere shortens: I-band narrows, H-zone narrows, A-band remains CONSTANT, Z-lines get closer. This is powered by ATP hydrolysis during cross-bridge cycling (power stroke). Reference: Guyton — Muscle Contraction.

questions.questionText: The band that remains constant during muscle contraction is the:
questions.optionB: A-band
questions.explanation: A-band (anisotropic, dark band) remains CONSTANT during contraction because it represents the full length of the thick (myosin) filaments, which do not change length. I-band (isotropic, light) and H-zone both DECREASE as thin filaments slide inward. Z-line to Z-line distance (sarcomere length) also decreases. Reference: Guyton — Sarcomere Changes.

questions.questionText: The source of Ca2+ for skeletal muscle contraction is:
questions.optionB: Sarcoplasmic reticulum (SR)
questions.explanation: In skeletal muscle, Ca2+ for contraction comes from the SR (via RyR1 receptors). In cardiac muscle, Ca2+ comes from BOTH extracellular (through L-type Ca2+ channels) AND SR (Ca2+-induced Ca2+ release via RyR2). In smooth muscle, Ca2+ comes from both extracellular and SR (via IP3 receptors). SERCA pump returns Ca2+ to SR for relaxation. Reference: Guyton — Calcium Sources.

questions.questionText: Myasthenia gravis is caused by antibodies against:
questions.optionB: Postsynaptic nicotinic ACh receptors
questions.explanation: Myasthenia gravis: autoimmune antibodies against postsynaptic nAChR at the NMJ → decreased EPP amplitude → fatigable weakness (worse with activity, better with rest). Ptosis (first symptom usually), diplopia, dysphagia, proximal weakness. Diagnosis: anti-AChR antibodies, decremental response on RNS, Tensilon (edrophonium) test. Treatment: AChE inhibitors (pyridostigmine), immunosuppression, thymectomy. Reference: Guyton — NMJ Disorders.

questions.questionText: The fastest conducting nerve fibers are:
questions.optionB: Aα fibers (myelinated, large diameter)
questions.explanation: Aα fibers: largest diameter (12-20 μm), heavily myelinated, fastest conduction (70-120 m/s). Functions: motor to skeletal muscle (α-motor neurons), proprioception (Ia, Ib afferents). Aβ: touch/pressure (30-70 m/s). Aδ: fast pain, temperature (5-30 m/s). B: preganglionic autonomic (3-15 m/s). C fibers: slowest (0.5-2 m/s), unmyelinated, slow pain, postganglionic autonomic. Reference: Guyton — Nerve Fiber Classification.

questions.questionText: Rigor mortis occurs because:
questions.optionB: ATP depletion prevents detachment of myosin from actin
questions.explanation: After death, ATP production stops. ATP is needed to: (1) detach myosin from actin (allow cross-bridge cycling), (2) pump Ca2+ back into SR (SERCA needs ATP). Without ATP, myosin heads remain permanently attached to actin → rigidity (rigor mortis). Begins 2-4 hours after death, peaks at 12-24 hours, resolves at 24-48 hours (as muscle proteins autolyze). Reference: Guyton — Rigor Mortis.

questions.questionText: The motor unit consists of:
questions.optionA: A single muscle fiber and its nerve
questions.optionB: A single motor neuron and ALL the muscle fibers it innervates
questions.explanation: Motor unit = one α-motor neuron + all the muscle fibers it innervates. Small motor units (eye muscles: 3-6 fibers/neuron) → fine control. Large motor units (gastrocnemius: 2000+ fibers/neuron) → powerful but coarse movements. All fibers in a motor unit are the same type (I or II). Motor unit recruitment follows Henneman's size principle (small units recruited first). Reference: Guyton — Motor Unit.

questions.questionText: Type I (slow-twitch/red) muscle fibers are characterized by:
questions.optionA: Fast contraction, anaerobic metabolism, easy fatigue
questions.optionB: Slow contraction, aerobic metabolism, fatigue-resistant
questions.explanation: Type I (slow-twitch, red): slow contraction speed, highly oxidative (many mitochondria, myoglobin → red color), fatigue-resistant, postural muscles (soleus). Type IIa (fast-twitch oxidative): fast, moderate fatigue resistance. Type IIb (fast-twitch glycolytic, white): fastest, glycolytic, fatigue quickly, powerful bursts (eye muscles, biceps). Marathon runners: more Type I. Sprinters: more Type II. Reference: Guyton — Muscle Fiber Types.
questions.conceptTag: Type I vs Type II fibers

questions.questionText: Tetanus (complete) in skeletal muscle occurs when:
questions.optionB: Stimuli frequency is so high that individual twitches fuse completely
questions.explanation: Complete tetanus: stimulus frequency so high that individual twitches fuse into a sustained smooth contraction. Force is 4-5x greater than a single twitch. Incomplete (unfused) tetanus: individual twitches partially fuse (can still see oscillations). Treppe (staircase phenomenon): progressive increase in force with repeated stimuli at low frequency. Reference: Guyton — Muscle Mechanics.

questions.questionText: In smooth muscle, contraction is initiated by:
questions.optionB: Ca2+-calmodulin complex activating MLCK (myosin light chain kinase)
questions.explanation: Smooth muscle lacks troponin. Ca2+ binds calmodulin → Ca2+-calmodulin complex activates MLCK → phosphorylates myosin light chain → allows cross-bridge cycling. Relaxation: MLCP (myosin light chain phosphatase) dephosphorylates myosin. Ca2+ sources: extracellular (voltage-gated and receptor-operated channels) and SR (via IP3 receptors). Reference: Guyton — Smooth Muscle.

questions.questionText: During isometric contraction:
questions.optionB: Muscle develops tension without changing length
questions.explanation: Isometric ('same length'): muscle generates force but does NOT shorten (e.g., holding a weight stationary, pushing against a wall). ATP is consumed. Isotonic ('same tension'): muscle shortens against a constant load (concentric) or lengthens under load (eccentric). Most movements are a combination. Isometric exercises increase blood pressure more than isotonic. Reference: Guyton — Muscle Mechanics.

questions.questionText: The miniature end plate potential (MEPP) is caused by:
questions.optionA: Release of a single quantum (vesicle) of ACh
questions.optionB: Opening of voltage-gated Na+ channels
questions.explanation: MEPPs are small (~0.4 mV), spontaneous depolarizations at the motor end plate caused by random release of single ACh vesicles (quanta, each containing ~10,000 ACh molecules). During normal transmission, an AP triggers synchronous release of ~200-300 quanta → EPP (~70 mV) → above threshold → muscle AP. Each MEPP is too small to reach threshold alone. This is the basis of quantal release theory. Reference: Guyton — NMJ Transmission.

questions.questionText: Denervation hypersensitivity refers to:
questions.optionB: Increased sensitivity of denervated tissue to its neurotransmitter
questions.explanation: After denervation, postsynaptic receptors upregulate (spread across the entire membrane instead of being confined to the end plate region). This causes increased sensitivity to neurotransmitter (ACh in the case of skeletal muscle). Clinically: succinylcholine after denervation/burns/immobilization → massive K+ release from upregulated nAChRs → hyperkalemia → cardiac arrest. Cannon's law of denervation. Reference: Guyton — Denervation.

questions.questionText: Wallerian degeneration occurs in:
questions.optionA: The nerve segment proximal to the injury
questions.optionB: The nerve segment distal to the injury
questions.optionC: Both proximal and distal segments equally
questions.explanation: Wallerian degeneration occurs in the DISTAL segment (and a small proximal portion) after nerve transection. The axon and myelin sheath break down, Schwann cells proliferate forming bands of Büngner (guide regeneration). Macrophages clear debris. The cell body undergoes chromatolysis (Nissl body dissolution). Regeneration: ~1-3 mm/day from proximal stump. Schwann cell tubes guide regrowing axons. Reference: Guyton — Nerve Degeneration.

questions.questionText: The length-tension relationship in skeletal muscle shows that maximum active tension is generated at:
questions.optionB: Optimal sarcomere length (~2.2 μm, resting length)
questions.explanation: At optimal resting length (~2.0-2.2 μm), maximum overlap between actin and myosin → maximum cross-bridge formation → maximum force. Too short: filaments overlap excessively, interfere with each other. Too stretched: insufficient overlap, fewer cross-bridges. This is the basis of the Frank-Starling mechanism in the heart (increased preload = increased stretch = increased force, up to a point). Reference: Guyton — Length-Tension.

questions.questionText: The neuromuscular blocking agent that acts by depolarization is:
questions.optionB: Succinylcholine
questions.explanation: Succinylcholine: depolarizing neuromuscular blocker. It binds nAChR → initial depolarization (fasciculations) → sustained depolarization → Phase I block (desensitization). Not degraded by AChE (broken down by plasma cholinesterase/pseudocholinesterase → short-acting). Tubocurarine, pancuronium, vecuronium: non-depolarizing (competitive) blockers. Reversed by neostigmine (AChE inhibitor). Reference: Guyton — NMJ Pharmacology.

questions.questionText: The energy source for the FIRST few seconds of intense muscle contraction is:
questions.optionB: Oxidative phosphorylation
questions.explanation: Energy sources in order: (1) First 5-8 seconds: stored ATP + creatine phosphate (phosphocreatine → creatine + Pi, catalyzed by creatine kinase, donates phosphate to ADP). (2) Next 1-2 minutes: anaerobic glycolysis (glucose → lactate). (3) Beyond 2 minutes: aerobic (oxidative phosphorylation — glucose, fatty acids, amino acids). Creatine phosphate provides the fastest energy replenishment. Reference: Guyton — Muscle Energetics.

questions.questionText: The role of tropomyosin in skeletal muscle at rest is to:
questions.optionB: Cover the active sites on actin, preventing myosin binding
questions.explanation: At rest, tropomyosin lies in the groove of the actin helix, physically blocking the myosin-binding sites. When Ca2+ binds troponin C → conformational change in troponin complex → tropomyosin shifts deeper into the groove → exposes active sites on actin → myosin heads bind → cross-bridge cycling. Troponin T binds tropomyosin, Troponin I inhibits interaction. Reference: Guyton — Regulatory Proteins.

questions.questionText: Henneman's size principle of motor unit recruitment states that:
questions.optionA: Large motor units are recruited first
questions.optionB: Small motor units are recruited first, then progressively larger ones
questions.optionC: All motor units are recruited simultaneously
questions.explanation: Henneman's size principle: motor units are recruited in order from smallest to largest. Small motor units (Type I, slow-twitch, low threshold) are recruited first → fine control, sustained activity. As more force is needed, larger motor units (Type II, fast-twitch, high threshold) are progressively recruited. This order ensures smooth gradation of force. Decruitment follows reverse order. Reference: Guyton — Motor Recruitment.

questions.questionText: The cardiac muscle differs from skeletal muscle in that cardiac muscle:
questions.optionB: Has a longer refractory period, preventing tetanus
questions.optionC: Does not require Ca2+ for contraction
questions.explanation: Cardiac muscle has a long absolute refractory period (250-300 ms, nearly as long as the contraction itself) → prevents tetanus → allows diastolic filling. Other differences: cardiac has intercalated discs (gap junctions), intrinsic automaticity (pacemaker cells), Ca2+-induced Ca2+ release (CICR), is involuntary, and cannot be recruited (all fibers contract). Reference: Guyton — Cardiac vs Skeletal Muscle.

questions.questionText: The compound muscle action potential (CMAP) is recorded during:
questions.optionB: Nerve conduction studies (NCS)
questions.explanation: CMAP is the sum of all motor unit action potentials recorded during NCS when a motor nerve is supramaximally stimulated. It measures motor nerve conduction velocity, distal latency, and amplitude. Reduced CMAP amplitude suggests axonal loss. Prolonged distal latency/slow conduction velocity suggests demyelination. EMG (electromyography) records individual motor unit potentials during voluntary contraction. Reference: Guyton — Electrodiagnosis.

questions.questionText: Botulinum toxin acts by:
questions.optionB: Preventing presynaptic ACh vesicle release (blocking SNARE proteins)
questions.explanation: Botulinum toxin (most potent biological toxin) is a zinc endopeptidase that cleaves SNARE proteins (synaptobrevin, SNAP-25, syntaxin) → prevents ACh vesicle fusion and release at the NMJ → flaccid paralysis. Clinically fatal in botulism (descending paralysis). Therapeutic uses (Botox): dystonia, spasticity, hyperhidrosis, cosmetic wrinkle reduction. Reference: Guyton — Presynaptic Toxins.

questions.questionText: The muscle spindle detects:
questions.optionB: Muscle length and rate of change of length
questions.explanation: Muscle spindle: stretch receptor detecting muscle LENGTH (static component, via type II/nuclear chain fibers) and RATE OF CHANGE of length (dynamic component, via type Ia/nuclear bag fibers). Afferent: Ia and II fibers. Efferent: γ-motor neurons (set spindle sensitivity). Golgi tendon organ (GTO) detects muscle TENSION (Ib afferents). Stretch reflex: Ia → α-motor neuron → muscle contraction (monosynaptic). Reference: Guyton — Proprioceptors.

questions.questionText: The stretch reflex (myotatic reflex) is a:
questions.optionB: Monosynaptic reflex
questions.explanation: The stretch reflex is the ONLY monosynaptic reflex in the body. Ia afferent from muscle spindle → direct synapse with α-motor neuron → contraction of the same (agonist) muscle. Example: knee jerk (patellar reflex, L3-L4). Simultaneously, Ia afferents also inhibit antagonist motor neurons via an inhibitory interneuron (reciprocal inhibition). All other reflexes (withdrawal, crossed extensor) are polysynaptic. Reference: Guyton — Stretch Reflex.

questions.questionText: Duchenne muscular dystrophy is caused by absence of:
questions.optionB: Dystrophin
questions.explanation: Duchenne MD: X-linked recessive, complete absence of dystrophin (DMD gene, Xp21). Dystrophin links the cytoskeleton (actin) to the extracellular matrix (via dystroglycan complex). Without it, membrane instability → muscle fiber necrosis → pseudohypertrophy (fat/fibrosis replaces muscle, especially calves). Gower's sign, progressive weakness, wheelchair by 12, death by 20s. Becker MD: reduced/abnormal dystrophin, milder. Reference: Guyton — Muscular Dystrophy.

questions.questionText: The Golgi tendon organ is stimulated by:
questions.optionB: Muscle tension (both passive and active)
questions.explanation: Golgi tendon organ (GTO): located at the muscle-tendon junction, encapsulated receptor detecting muscle TENSION. Afferent fiber: Ib. When tension is too high → Ib inhibitory reflex → inhibition of agonist motor neuron, facilitation of antagonist → protective mechanism against excessive force/tendon rupture. This is the inverse myotatic reflex. GTO is more sensitive to active contraction than passive stretch. Reference: Guyton — Golgi Tendon Organ.
